skill.name: web3-testing
skill.description: Guides smart contract testing with Hardhat and Foundry, including unit tests, fuzzing, gas checks, mainnet forks, coverage, and verification workflows.
skill.summary: Build and review Solidity test suites with Hardhat, Foundry, forking, fuzzing, and coverage guidance.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, and secret patterns, but the reviewed file is a documentation skill made of Hardhat and Foundry examples. No prompt injection, malware, credential exfiltration, or destructive intent was found; the remaining risk is that users may run CLI commands or configure private keys and RPC/API secrets from the examples.

medium_findings.title: Environment-Based Private Key and API Key Configuration
medium_findings.description: The Hardhat examples read MAINNET_RPC_URL, PRIVATE_KEY, COINMARKETCAP_API_KEY, ETHERSCAN_API_KEY, and fork RPC values from environment variables. This is normal for blockchain tooling, but users must avoid exposing private keys in shared projects, logs, or CI settings.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable references are explicit and include private key and API key names. The context is legitimate test configuration, so the issue is handling risk rather than confirmed exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: External CLI and CI Command Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill includes examples that run Hardhat coverage, Hardhat verification, npm install, compile, test, and coverage commands. These are expected for this skill, but users should run them only in trusted repositories after reviewing dependencies and configuration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plainly visible in documentation examples and match the skill purpose. They are not executed by the skill itself, so the risk depends on user execution context.

medium_findings.title: Mainnet Forking and Account Impersonation Examples
medium_findings.description: The examples show mainnet fork RPC use, hardhat_reset, hardhat_impersonateAccount, and live contract interactions. These are standard test techniques, but they create network dependence and can be misunderstood as permission to use production secrets or signers.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network and impersonation calls are concrete, but they are presented as local fork testing patterns. No evidence shows unauthorized access or real transaction signing.

low_findings.title: Relative Import Flagged as Path Traversal False Positive
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged ../src/Token.sol, but this is a normal Solidity relative import in a Foundry test example. No file read, write, upload, or user-controlled path handling is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a static import statement inside an example test contract. There is no dynamic filesystem operation or traversal sink.

low_findings.title: Blocker Keyword and Weak Crypto Flags Are Contextual False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner reported C2 keywords and weak cryptographic algorithm markers on smart contract testing lines. Review found testing terminology, contract addresses, DAI references, and describe blocks, but no command-and-control behavior or weak cryptography implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are ordinary Solidity and JavaScript test examples. I found no semantic evidence of C2 infrastructure, encryption downgrade, or cryptographic misuse.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secret-Backed Blockchain Tooling Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples reference private keys, RPC URLs, and third-party API keys through environment variables. This pattern is common, but it requires careful secret storage and CI scoping.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The secret names are explicit and security-relevant. The documentation context lowers the severity because no code sends them to an attacker-controlled endpoint.

dangerous_patterns.title: Networked Forking and Verification Workflows
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples depend on RPC endpoints, Etherscan verification, and Codecov upload actions. These are expected workflows, but they introduce external service trust and network data sharing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The external service interactions are visible and legitimate for Web3 testing. No evidence shows hidden exfiltration or unexpected destinations beyond common developer services.

content.user_title: Test Smart Contracts with Hardhat and Foundry
content.value_statement: Smart contract teams need repeatable tests before deploying protocol changes. This skill provides practical Hardhat and Foundry patterns for unit tests, forks, fuzzing, gas checks, coverage, and verification.
actual_capabilities: Shows Hardhat configuration for Solidity compilation, gas reporting, coverage, forking, and verification.
Provides unit test examples for deployment, transfers, events, reverts, time changes, and gas assertions.
Explains Foundry test patterns including fixtures, fuzzing, cheatcodes, fork tests, and account impersonation.
Covers mainnet fork setup for testing against existing contracts such as Uniswap and DAI.
Includes CI examples for installing dependencies, compiling contracts, running tests, and uploading coverage.
limitations: It is a guidance skill, not a complete audited test suite for a specific protocol.
Examples use placeholder addresses, RPC URLs, and constructor values that must be adapted safely.
It does not replace a formal smart contract security audit or economic risk review.
Mainnet fork examples require trusted RPC providers and careful handling of secrets.

use_cases.title: Launch a Solidity Test Suite
use_cases.description: Set up Hardhat or Foundry tests for deployment, transfers, revert paths, events, and time-based behavior.

use_cases.title: Validate DeFi Integrations
use_cases.description: Use mainnet forking to test interactions with existing token and router contracts before integration release.

use_cases.title: Improve Release Confidence
use_cases.description: Add coverage, gas checks, fuzzing, and CI test steps to make contract changes easier to review.

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic Unit Tests
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a simple contract
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me write Hardhat unit tests for my Solidity contract. Cover deployment, ownership, transfers, events, and expected reverts.

prompt_templates.title: Add Foundry Fuzz Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this contract behavior and propose Foundry fuzz tests with clear assumptions, invariants, and edge cases.

prompt_templates.title: Design Mainnet Fork Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a mainnet fork test suite for this DeFi integration. Include RPC setup, contract fixtures, impersonation boundaries, and assertions.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Test Coverage Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate my smart contract test strategy for missing risk coverage. Check access control, reentrancy, gas limits, fork tests, CI, and verification steps.

output_examples.input: I need tests for an ERC-20 transfer function.
output: A suggested Hardhat test plan covering successful transfers, insufficient balance reverts, zero-address checks, and Transfer event assertions.
A fixture structure that deploys the token once and reuses clean state across tests.
A short list of edge cases to add before release.

output_examples.input: I want to test my protocol against Uniswap on a fork.
output: A fork testing checklist covering fixed block selection, RPC configuration, token balances, router calls, and deterministic assertions.
Guidance to keep private keys out of tests and use local impersonation only on forked state.
Recommended CI safeguards for tests that depend on external RPC providers.

best_practices: Use deterministic fixtures and fixed fork blocks for repeatable test results.
Keep private keys and RPC credentials in scoped environment secrets, never in source files.
Combine unit tests, fuzz tests, fork tests, gas checks, and coverage before release.
anti_patterns: Do not use production private keys in local or CI test configurations.
Do not rely only on mainnet fork tests when isolated unit tests can verify core logic.
Do not treat high coverage percentages as proof that protocol risk is fully tested.

faq.question: Does this skill run tests automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides examples and guidance. The user or agent must run commands in the target project.

faq.question: Which frameworks does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Hardhat and Foundry patterns for Solidity testing, forking, fuzzing, gas checks, and coverage.

faq.question: Can it replace a smart contract audit?
faq.answer: No. It improves testing coverage, but it does not replace expert security review or economic analysis.

faq.question: Are mainnet fork tests required?
faq.answer: No. They are useful for integration behavior, but unit tests should cover core contract logic first.

faq.question: How should secrets be handled?
faq.answer: Use environment variables or CI secret stores with minimum required scope. Never commit private keys or API keys.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
